questions.question: Why is it risky to describe a distributed KV store as simply 'eventually consistent' and stop there?
options: Because storage systems are required to expose SQL joins for every key.
Because clients still need to know what anomalies are possible and what write durability was actually acknowledged.
Because eventual consistency means reads can never return fresh data.
Because all distributed KV stores must elect a global leader.
questions.explanation: A useful answer names the real contract, not just a broad label.

questions.question: What makes hot keys dangerous in a partitioned KV store?
options: They concentrate work on one shard or replica set even when average fleet load looks healthy.
They prevent replication from writing to disk at all.
They force every client to use synchronous cross-region writes.
They only matter when values exceed the maximum object size.
questions.explanation: Skewed keys can overwhelm one owner set long before total cluster capacity is exhausted.

questions.question: Why is replica placement across racks or AZs more important than just replica count?
options: Because three replicas on one host protect against all correlated failures.
Because durability depends on independence of failure domains, not only on the raw number of copies.
Because replica placement only affects read latency and not safety.
Because quorum math stops working once replicas span more than one rack.
questions.explanation: Copies in the same failure domain can disappear together.

questions.question: What is the clearest role of anti-entropy in a leaderless KV store?
options: It permanently replaces write-ahead logging on every replica.
It reconciles missed or divergent updates after failures or temporary partitions.
It guarantees zero-latency reads during failover.
It removes the need for partition maps.
questions.explanation: Background repair is what makes temporary divergence heal over time.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that repair debt is becoming a serving risk?
options: Average HTTP response body size.
Replica divergence age or repair backlog bytes.
Only total logical key count.
The number of code deploys this week.
questions.explanation: Repair lag directly measures how long inconsistency can persist.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with exposing multiple consistency levels to clients?
options: It lets clients pay for freshness when needed, but pushes more semantic complexity onto application teams.
It guarantees all reads are both cheapest and freshest.
It removes the need for replica repair entirely.
It prevents the service from supporting deletes.
questions.explanation: Flexibility is useful, but it increases the chance of misuse.

questions.question: Why do LSM-based engines fit many distributed KV stores well?
options: They make compaction unnecessary once data is replicated.
They favor high write throughput by turning random writes into sequential flush and compaction work.
They enforce strong serializable transactions for every partition.
They require no read amplification under any workload.
questions.explanation: LSM trees are a common fit for write-heavy replicated storage, with compaction as the cost.

questions.question: Which design change most directly helps when a single configuration key becomes globally hot?
options: Increase value size so clients request it less often.
Add replication-aware read fan-out or an external cache for that key rather than relying on one owner path.
Disable observability to reduce overhead.
Move all writes to a weekly batch job.
questions.explanation: The right fix targets read concentration, not unrelated parts of the system.
